title: Computer Vision Object Sorter
description: Build a robotic system that uses computer vision to identify, classify, and sort objects by type, colour, or shape. Integrate a camera with a robotic arm or conveyor mechanism to create a working automated sorting cell. This project combines vision processing, object detection, and robot control into a practical industrial application.
whyItMatters: Automated sorting is one of the highest-value applications of robotic vision — used in recycling plants, food processing, e-commerce fulfilment, and quality inspection. The vision-to-action pipeline you build here (detect object, classify it, decide where it goes, pick and place it) is the same pipeline used in commercial sorting systems worldwide. In Australia, vision-based sorting is critical for the mining industry (ore sorting), agriculture (fruit grading), and the rapidly growing e-commerce logistics sector.
skillsLearned: Object detection using OpenCV or deep learning
Hand-eye calibration (camera to robot coordinate transform)
prerequisites: OpenCV fundamentals (image loading, colour spaces, thresholding)

steps.title: Set up the vision system
steps.description: Mount a camera (USB webcam or Intel RealSense) above the workspace looking down. Calibrate the camera intrinsics using a checkerboard pattern. Establish the transform from camera coordinates to robot/workspace coordinates (hand-eye calibration or manual measurement). Verify by pointing the camera at an object at a known position and confirming the calculated coordinates match.
steps.tips: A fixed overhead camera is simpler than a wrist-mounted camera for a first project. Use good, diffuse lighting to minimise shadows and reflections.

steps.title: Implement object detection and classification
steps.description: Write a vision pipeline that: captures an image, detects objects on the workspace (using background subtraction, contour detection, or a trained YOLO model), classifies each object by category (colour, shape, or type), and estimates the object's position in workspace coordinates. Test with at least 3 categories of objects (e.g. red circles, blue squares, green triangles, or different product types).
steps.tips: For colour-based sorting, convert to HSV colour space for robust colour detection. For shape-based sorting, use contour analysis (area, perimeter, aspect ratio, number of vertices).

steps.title: Design the sorting logic
steps.description: Define sorting bins or target locations for each category. Implement the decision logic: for each detected object, determine which bin it belongs to based on classification. Handle ambiguous cases (low confidence, partially occluded objects) by sending them to a 'manual review' bin. Implement a queue system if multiple objects are detected simultaneously.
steps.tips: Process objects in order of accessibility — pick the easiest-to-reach object first. Consider the optimal bin layout to minimise robot travel distance.

steps.title: Integrate with robot control
steps.description: Connect the vision system to a robot arm (real or simulated). For each detected object: compute the pick pose from the object's position, generate a trajectory to approach and grasp the object, pick it up, move to the correct bin, and place it. Implement the full pick-detect-sort-place cycle as an automated loop.
steps.tips: Add a verification step after picking — check that the gripper actually has the object (using force sensing or a camera check) before moving to the bin.

steps.title: Optimise and benchmark
steps.description: Run a full sorting session with 20+ objects of mixed types. Measure classification accuracy (correct bin assignment), pick success rate, cycle time per object, and total throughput. Identify bottlenecks and optimise — is vision processing, motion planning, or the physical pick the limiting factor?
steps.tips: Overlap vision processing with robot motion — while the arm is placing one object, the camera should be detecting the next. This parallelism can nearly double throughput.

deliverables: Classification accuracy report (confusion matrix across object categories)
System performance metrics: cycle time, throughput, success rate
Technical documentation covering vision pipeline, calibration, and robot integration

rubric.description: Objects are correctly classified into the right category at least 95% of the time
Objects are successfully picked and placed in the correct bin at least 90% of the time
System achieves a reasonable cycle time (under 15 seconds per object for simulation, under 30 for real hardware)
System handles variation in object position, orientation, and lighting conditions
Clean separation between vision, decision, and robot control modules with well-defined interfaces
